coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon/titanium oxide quantum dots from lignocellulose-rich mandarin orange peel extract via microwave irradiation: Synthesis, characterization and bio-imaging application
coredata.dc:creator: Rajendran, Kalimuthu
Ganesan, Sivarasan
Manikandan, Velu
Sivaselvam, Subramani
AlSalhi, Mohamad S.
Asemi, Nassar N.
Angayarkanni, Jayaraman
Rajendiran, Nagappan
Lo, Huang-Mu
coredata.dc:description: A nanohybrid prepared from the lignocellulosic residue is a feasible approach to synthesize blue light emitting fluorescent doped TiO2 quantum dot nanocomposite (C-TiO2 QDs) by microwave techniques using Mandarin orange (Citrus reticulata) peel powder with titanium isopropoxide precursors. With a greater orange peel colloidal medium, the structure of the TiO2-NPs changed from a mixture of rutile and anatase phases to exclusively the anatase phase. The optical and morphological properties of as-prepared C-TiO2 QDs were characterized by HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–visible, PL spectra, DLS, and Zeta potential techniques. The reaction condition was optimized by changing substrate composition, pH, and reaction time. C-TiO2 QDs exhibit outstanding stability at pH 7 and remain sustained for at least 180 days without aggregation. As prepared C-TiO2 QDs have distinct emission and excitation activities with an average particle size of 2.8 nm. Cell viability was performed on normal L929 cells, where it showed excellent biocompatibility (<90 %) even at the concentration of 200 μg/mL after 24 h treatment. Additionally, the synthesized C-TiO2 QDs were used with L929 cells as a fluorescent probe for bio-imaging applications. The results revealed that neither of the cell lines' morphologies had significantly changed, proving the biocompatibility of the synthetic C-TiO2 QDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biosynthesis
Citrus peel
Lignocellulosic biomass
Microwave-assisted synthesis
Carbon/TiO2 NPs
In-vitro L929 cell viability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots functionalized TiO2
Morphological and functional characterization of C-TiO2 QDs
Influence of pH and stability of C-TiO2 QDs
In-vitro biocompatibility and bio-imaging property of C-TiO2 QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of (a) Pure TiO2; (b) CQDs; (c-d) C-TiO2 QDs.
Physiochemical properties of the C-TiO2 QDs. (a) The X-ray diffraction pattern of CQDs and (b) C-TiO2 QDs. c) FT-IR spectrum of C-TiO2 QDs.
As-prepared C-TiO2 QDs quantum properties of (a) Different excitation spectrum (b) Contour image of corresponding different excitation and (d) TCSPC lifetime of C-TiO2 QDs.
(a) Emission spectra of C-TiO2 QDs at different pH values, (b) Zeta potential, (c) DLS, and (d) Photoluminance stability of C-TiO2 QDs under UV-light irradiation.

Biocompatibility of C-TiO2 QDs on L929 cells. (a) Cell viability, (b) ROS production in L929 cells with different concentrations of C-TiO2 QDs. (c) Morphological observation of control and treated cells using FESEM, Phase contrast, and fluorescent microscope.
Different synthesis route with precursors and application of titanium-based quantum dots.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots = NCQDs; Carbon dots = CD; Fluorine-doped carbon nitride quantum dots = CNF.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon/titanium oxide quantum dots from lignocellulose-rich mandarin orange peel extract via microwave irradiation: Synthesis, characterization and bio-imaging application

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both are contributed as the first author equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanohybrid prepared from the lignocellulosic residue is a feasible approach to synthesize blue light emitting fluorescent doped TiO2 quantum dot nanocomposite (C-TiO2 QDs) by microwave techniques using Mandarin orange (Citrus reticulata) peel powder with titanium isopropoxide precursors. With a greater orange peel colloidal medium, the structure of the TiO2-NPs changed from a mixture of rutile and anatase phases to exclusively the anatase phase. The optical and morphological properties of as-prepared C-TiO2 QDs were characterized by HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–visible, PL spectra, DLS, and Zeta potential techniques. The reaction condition was optimized by changing substrate composition, pH, and reaction time. C-TiO2 QDs exhibit outstanding stability at pH 7 and remain sustained for at least 180 days without aggregation. As prepared C-TiO2 QDs have distinct emission and excitation activities with an average particle size of 2.8 nm. Cell viability was performed on normal L929 cells, where it showed excellent biocompatibility (<90 %) even at the concentration of 200 μg/mL after 24 h treatment. Additionally, the synthesized C-TiO2 QDs were used with L929 cells as a fluorescent probe for bio-imaging applications. The results revealed that neither of the cell lines' morphologies had significantly changed, proving the biocompatibility of the synthetic C-TiO2 QDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Many nanomaterials are shows excellent optical properties, such as titanium dioxide, cerium oxide, nickel oxide and zinc oxide in the field of biomedical applications [1–6]. Among these TiO2 is the most widely recognized material in the current scenario, because of its cost efficiency, high chemical stability, various morphologies and tuneable particle size. Owing to these versatile properties it has been used in a wide range of applications including electronic properties, photocatalytic activity and biological applications [7–9]. Additionally, TiO2 nanoparticles applied many environmental applications including antimicrobial agents and photocatalysts for clearing various microorganisms [10,11]. They are also, used as a white pigment preservative in the production of sunscreens, face creams, food additives, antimicrobial agents, pharmaceuticals products, and various environmental water remediations [12–14]. However, the disadvantage of raw TiO2 in electronic excitation can only be overcome by UV light irradiation. To improve their biological activity, various metal oxide nanoparticles, including Carbon. iron, copper, silver, and zinc, were doped with TiO2 NPs [15–18].

By attempting different adequate methods for the synthesis of TiO2, including hydrothermal [19] and chemical synthesis [20], allotropes of TiO2 and their hybrid nanostructures were synthesized via sol-gel [21] and other major methods such as laser ablation [22], thermal plasma processing [23], ball mill [24,25] and biosynthesis process [26]. i.e., According to Venkatasubbu et al., 2016, the TiO2 and ZnO NPs are effective antimicrobial agents against valuable pathogens such as Shigella flexneri, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Salmonella typhi, [27]. Nica et al. 2017 have developed TiO2 NPs with pulmonary fibroblasts, pathogenic microorganisms, and human dermal fibroblasts [28].

TiO2/ZnO nanocomposites were made using a non-hydrolytic sol-gel method for antibacterial activity [29]. Si-doped carbon dots for bioimaging applications were accomplished from hydroquinone using silicon tetrachloride [30]. Non-metals like nitrogen, sulfur, and boron have recently been employed as doping agents to increase the biocompatibility of carbon dots. Gadolinium-doped Carbon dots have recently been produced by Radek Zboril and his colleagues as dual fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) probes [31]. Cu-doped Carbon dots have been created by Quan Xu et al. as fluorescent sensing probes to find Fe3+ ions [32]. Doping drugs, in contrast, exhibit huge hydrodynamic diameters, intricate synthetic processes, and systemic toxicity at increasing concentrations [33–35]. There are currently very few reports on research into the synthesis and uses of TiO2-doped Carbon dots. For instance, CQDs-TiO2 nanostructures have been synthesized in situ and have been shown to exhibit good photocatalytic activity for dye degradation by Sathish Kumar et al. [36]. In addition, a few of the TiO2-based nanomaterials and their environmental applications were listed in Table 1 .

Therefore, in the present investigation, Carbon and TiO2−based quantum dots are synthesized by simple microwave irradiation technique. As-prepared C-TiO2 QDs are well characterized and their biocompatibility is evaluated using mouse fibroblast L929 cells. Fibroblasts are the most common cells of connective tissue in animals. The fibroblast cells synthesize the extracellular matrix which produces the structural framework for animal tissues. The higher optical property of C-TiO2 QDs is evaluated for bio-imaging applications. The C-TiO2 QDs performed as a good fluorescent probe with no photobleaching effect. This study would unravel the potential and future biological applications of C-TiO2 QDs.

Titanium tetrachloride, isopropyl alcohol, toluene, ammonia, sodium hydroxide, PBS buffer, and H2DCFDA, were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All the other chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade obtained from Himedia, India, and were used without further purification. The 18.2 MΩ cm−1 , Ultrapure water was used in all experimental processes.

Titanium isopropoxide was prepared by adopting a previously reported procedure with slight modification [37]. 0.66 g of Titanium tetrachloride (0.0032 M) was slowly added to 15 mL of isopropyl alcohol and saturated with ammonia at 5 °C. To eliminate the excess isopropyl alcohol, a filter, and extracts were mixed and concentrated. After adding 100 mL of toluene to dilute the solution underwent a 2 h ammonia environment. Following the removal of the extra precipitate, the filtrate was dried at 80 °C under reduced pressure. There was a solid-like gummy. About obtained yield, 1 g of resulting crude titanium isopropoxide was dissolved in 50 mL of boiling isopropyl alcohol, and upon cooling, a mass of colorless crystals was revealed. Finally, at room temperature, the crystalline complex was dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots functionalized TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh mandarin orange (Citrus reticulata) fruit peel wastes were collected from the local Wufeng fruit market in Taichung, Taiwan and oven dried at 105 °C for 24 h. The 1 g of dry mandarin peel powder was thoroughly mixed with 10 mL of titanium isopropoxide in an ethanol medium in double-distilled water in a 100 mL beaker. After 5 min of heating in a domestic microwave (700 W), the mixture's color changed from opaque to dark brown, signifying the carbonization of the reactant. To dissolve the C-TiO2 QDs, 30 mL of ultrapure water was added after the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature. For 15 min, the resultant dark brown solution was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm. The brown solution with a clear supernatant was collected. The crystal-clear aqueous solution was then kept at 4 °C for additional examination.

Using an X-Ray diffractometer, the structural analysis of nanomaterials was investigated (XRD, Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan). Using a JEOL JEM 2100 at 200 Kv, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were taken. Agilent Cary 660 (USA) was used to obtain the functional group analysis of the samples using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) apparatus is used for Particle size measurements. With the use of an LS-50B Luminescence Spectrometer, PL emission measurements were carried out (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). A TU-1810 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer was used to measure UV–Vis absorption (Pgeneral, China). Utilizing the Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) Spectrometer, the PL lifetime decay profile was determined (FLS920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

The MTT colorimetric assay was employed to conduct a cell viability experiment as previously reported [38]. Initially, 2 × 105 L929 cells per well were seeded in a 96-well plate containing 200 μl of MEM (Minimal Essential Medium) medium supplemented with 10 % FBS and allowed to adhere for a period of 24 in the CO2 incubator. Subsequently, different concentrations of C-TiO2 QDs (0–200 μg/mL) were administered to the samples for durations of 24 h and further incubated with MTT dye (5 mg mL−1) for 4 h. After treatment, the media components were removed, and added with 200 μL of DMSO was to dissolve the formazan crystal. Cell viability is measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader, and is correlated with the intensity of the formazan crystal generated. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

The H2DCFDA dye from Invitrogen was employed to determine the ROS generation inside the cells. The L929 cells were seeded on 24-well plates and irradiated to C-TiO2 QDs at concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μg/mL for 12 h. Further, the cells were washed with PBS before being exposed to 10 m of H2DCFDA in the dark for 30 min. Then, a Biotek spectrofluorometer was used to examine the amount of DCF fluorescence at wavelengths of 498 and 520 nm for excitation and emission, respectively. For each experiment, the test was replicated three times.

An FE-SEM analysis was conducted to evaluate the biocompatibility of as-prepared C-TiO2 QDs. Cells were seeded on a glass coverslip and incubated for 24 h in a CO2 incubator. Then the cells were exposed to a 100 μg/mL concentration of C-TiO2 QDs for 12 h. the cells without any treatment are taken as control. Following this, the cells were incubated in 3 % glutaraldehyde for 3 h, washed with PBS, and then with 0.1 % osmium tetroxide for an additional 3 h. Finally, the cells were washed with a gradient of ethanol from 30 to 100 % and gold-sputtered for 30 s. The morphological changes in the cells were observed using FE-SEM analysis.

L929 cells were grown in DMEM with 10 % FBS, 100 U/mL penicillin, and 100 μg/mL streptomycin. To evaluate the bioimaging property of C-TiO2, 200 μg/mL of the sample is added to the respective wells and left to incubate for 6 h. The wells without C-TiO2 QDs were taken as the control. Following the treatment, the wells were rinsed with PBS (pH -7.2) and examined under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse Ts2R).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and functional characterization of C-TiO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and shape of the C-TiO2 QDs under optimal conditions were observed by HR-TEM images with different magnifications. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a-d) clearly shows that C-TiO2 QDs exhibit well-dispersed quasi-spherical nano-dots and had a narrow size distribution with average diameters of 3 ± 0.3 nm and an average size of 0.56 nm [39,40]. Powder XRD measurement provided additional evidence of the synthesized C-TiO2 QDs' crystalline nature. A large peak appeared at 22.3° assigned to the (002) plane in the powder XRD pattern of pure CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2a. Additionally, it is found that the interlayer spacing (d) value of CQDs is 0.42 nm, which is slightly similar to graphite nanomaterial (0.33–0.34 nm). The weak crystalline nature of CQDs could be the cause of the disparity. After doping with TiO2, the C-TiO2 QDs showed six distinct peaks around 33°, 48°, 57°, 68°, 76° and 85° in the 2θ region as shown in Fig. 2b, which correspond to the plane of (101), (112), (211), (103), (222) and (224), respectively. Peak locations for typical TiO2 anatase (JCPDS 00–021-1272) and CQDS-TiO2 patterns were identical [26]. The peaks obtained from the sample were intense and sharp, indicating high crystallinity. The functional groups present in the C-TiO2 QDs were confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy and the obtained peak position of the C-TiO2 QDs is shown in Fig. 2c. The peaks appeared at 3401, 1581, 1401 and 1228 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching mode of –OH, NH2, CO, −NH and CO functional groups are present on the CQDs, respectively [41]. Also, a broad peak was observed at 750 cm−1 which could be due to the stretching frequency of TiO2.

C-TiO2 QDs excitation and emission spectra were recorded under ideal conditions, as shown in Fig. 3a. The solid emission peak, which was seen at 537 nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 127 nm when stimulated at 410 nm, provided evidence of the presence of blue emission properties in C-TiO2 QDs. Fig. 3b depicts the corresponding solution under UV light irradiation at 365 nm and daylight, where the solution exhibits blue emission characteristics. These emission properties might be attributed to emissive defects and decreased quantum confinement. A weak shoulder at 428 nm corresponds to the π–π* transition of the CO band and n–π* transitions of the conjugated CC bands.

The emission properties of C-TiO2 QDs at different excitation wavelengths were also demonstrated as shown in Fig. 3b. As the excitation wavelength was between 330 and 400 nm, the intensity of emission increased towards the longer wavelength. Besides, the corresponding 3-dimensional contour plot Fig. 3c of the excitation versus emission curves shows a maximum intensity between 330 and 400 nm for excitation and 530–550 nm for emission. The quantum yield was calculated for C-TiO2 QDs and found to be about 11.2 % (λex = 350 nm) concerning quinine sulfate (54 %) as the standard. Fig. 3d displays the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) measurement of C-TiO2 QDs with a bi-exponential function that successfully fits the decay curve [42]. The peak's fit was tested with lifetime χ2 values between 1 and 1.15, and it was found that the associated amplitudes α1 and α2 were 4.23 ns (86.51) and 13.42 ns (13.49) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH and stability of C-TiO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The C-TiO2 QDs were dispersed in water at the pH range of 4 to 12, and the changes in the intensity of fluorescence were recorded to determine the stability of prepared C-TiO2 QDs at various pH levels (Fig. 4a). The fluorescence intensity gradually increases with pH 4 to 7. The fluorescent intensity gradually declines when the pH was increased above pH 7. The decrease in the fluorescent intensity in pH higher than 7 could be due to the de-protonation. The pH 7 with higher intensity is taken as optimum pH for C-TiO2 QDs. From the zeta potential measurements, the synthesized C-TiO2 QDs exhibited a negative zeta potential value of −13.1 mV at pH 7 (Fig. 4b). This could be eventually a result of carboxyl group (COO-) present on the surface [43,44]. Also, the prepared C-TiO2 QDs displayed good stability for 6 months at 4 °C. The DLS analysis of C-TiO2 QDs further confirms the hydrodynamic radius with ~2.6 nm, which is slightly greater than the results obtained from TEM results (Fig. 4c).

This is explained by the fact that DLS considers the hydrodynamic size distribution of both core particles and functional groups over the surface, whereas, in the TEM analysis, only the metallic particle core is measured. Furthermore, the photostability of synthesized C-TiO2 QDs was demonstrated under continuous irradiation of a 500 W xenon lamp for 5 h, where the obtained results showed no significant changes in the intensity of fluorescence. This result confirms the existence of high photostability of C-TiO2 QDs (Fig. 4d). could be due to the existence of high photostability of synthesized C-TiO2 QDs. The higher photo-stability and fluorescent intensity at pH 7 of the prepared C-TiO2 QDs are further explored as a fluorescent probe for the Bio-imaging application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro biocompatibility and bio-imaging property of C-TiO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: An assessment of cell viability was done to evaluate the biocompatibility of synthesized C-TiO2 QDs using mouse fibroblast L929 cells (Fig. 5 ). The C-TiO2 QDs demonstrated great biocompatibility after a 24 h treatment with above 90 % cell viability for 200 μg/mL (Fig. 5a). This result was further evident by the light microscopy images (Fig. 5c). Moreover, C-TiO2 QDs did not induce intracellular ROS production indicating non-toxicity to the cells (Fig. 5b). The morphological analysis of cells by FE-SEM also indicates the biocompatibility of C-TiO2 QDs (Fig. 5c). Both the control and treated cells show intact cell structure without any cell blebbing and shrinking, due to C-TiO2 QDs' excellent water solubility and biocompatibility, we further evaluated their bio-imaging property. The L929 cells were incubated with 100 μg/mL of C-TiO2 QDs for 6 h and the cells were imaged using fluorescence microscopy to analyze the interaction of L929 cells and C-TiO2 QDs. The fluorescent microscopy analysis showed the efficient uptake of C-TiO2 QDs by L929 cells (Fig. 5c). The C-TiO2 QDs effectively stains the cytoplasm of the cells and shows good bioimaging property. The internalization of C-TiO2 QDs within the cell might be attributed to its small size, enabling it to penetrate the cell membrane through its pores, as well as by endocytosis. The prepared C-TiO2 QDs showed good biocompatibility and water solubility. These findings validate the synthesis of C-TiO2 QDs as a promising agent for bioimaging and the delivery of drugs.

This study described a compact, affordable, and efficient method for microwave-based synthesis of blue light-emitting fluorescent C-TiO2 QDs using lignocellulose-rich mandarin peel and titanium isopropoxide. The microwave irradiation reaction was accomplished within 5 min. The optical and morphological properties of as-prepared C-TiO2 QDs were characterized via HR-TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–visible, PL spectra, TCSPC, DLS, and Zeta potential techniques were used completely. The synthesized C-TiO2 QDs exhibit excellent stability at pH 7 and remain stable for >180 days without aggregation. With an average particle size of 2.8 nm, as-prepared C-TiO2 QDs have different excitation and emission properties. Cell viability was performed on normal L929 cells, where it showed excellent biocompatibility even at the concentration of 100 μg/mL after 24 h treatment. Besides, the prepared C-TiO2 QDs were utilized as fluorescent probes for bio-imaging applications using L929 cells. The results revealed that no of the cell lines' morphologies had significantly changed, showing the biocompatibility of the synthetic C-TiO2 QDs. Through the MTT assay and microscopic investigations, the cell viability of CQDs was studied in Vero and A549 cells, where it demonstrated higher biocompatibility nature on both cell lines with minimal morphological modifications. Additionally, fluorescence microscopy was employed to image A549 cells employing the suitable CQDs as a fluorescent probe. The investigation of both environmental and biological samples could thus be performed using them as fluorescent probes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors Dr N.R and K.R thank SERB-EEQ /2018/001092, Government of India for financial support. Huang-Mu Lo reports financial support was provided by the National Science and Technology Council, Taiwan, R.O.C. The authors are grateful to the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2023R68), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
